coredata.dc:title: Graphitic carbon nitride nanodots: As reductant for the synthesis of silver nanoparticles and its biothiols biosensing application
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Qiujun
Wang, Haiyan
Liu, Yalan
Hou, Yuxin
Li, Haitao
Zhang, Youyu
coredata.dc:description: The graphitic carbon nitride nanodots (g-C3N4-dots) were synthesized by a simple electrochemical “tailoring” process from bulk graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) under alkaline solution for the first time. Compared with the bulk g-C3N4, the novel g-C3N4-dots not only exhibit enhanced fluorescence and excellent dispersion stability in water but also show the reducibility for the reduction of Ag+ to AgNPs at 60°C. The biothiols can bound with Ag+ through formation of biothiol-Ag+ complex to consume the Ag+ and act as capping agent to prevent the growth of AgNPs, which cause the decrease of the absorption peak of the AgNPs. Therefore, an optical sensor was developed for the detection of biothiols based on the change of the plasmon resonance absorption peak of the AgNPs. The proposed method exhibits excellent sensitivity and selectivity to biothiols with low detection limit for cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy), and glutathione (GSH) with 11.5, 16.1, and 15.5nM, respectively. This method also has been successfully applied for the detection of biothiols in human serum with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphitic carbon nitride nanodots
Reductant
Silver nanoparticles
Biothiols
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of AgNPs with g-C3N4-dots as reducing agent
Detection of biothiols with g-C3N4-dots as reducing agent
The analytical performance of the sensor
Analysis of Cys in human serum samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21275051 , 21475043 ), Doctoral Fund of Ministry of Education of China (No: 20134306110006 ), Science and Technology Department of Hunan Province (No. 2014FJ6034 ) and the Hunan Provincial Innovation Foundation for Postgraduate ( CX2014B223 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FT-IR spectra of bulk g-C3N4 and g-C3N4-dots. (B) Typical TEM image of as-synthesized g-C3N4-dots. (C) UV–vis spectra of the Ag+ solution incubating with and without g-C3N4-dots under 60°C for 30min (D) the typical TEM image of as-synthesized AgNPs.
UV–vis spectra as-synthesized AgNPs in the absence and presence of different concentrations of Cys.
Illustration of the synthesis of g-C3N4-dots from bulk g-C3N4 and the detection of biothiols.

(A) UV–vis spectra of the AgNPs with different concentrations of Cys. (B) The relationship between the absorbance decrease rate and the concentration of Cys. Inset shows the calibration curve at low concentrations. The error bars were magnified by a factor of five.
The absorbance change rate of the AgNPs in the presence of biothiols and other compounds. (The concentration of Cys, Hcy, GSH and BSA is 0.1µM, and the concentration of other compounds is 10µM.).
Results of the determination of Cys in serum.

head.ce:title: Graphitic carbon nitride nanodots: As reductant for the synthesis of silver nanoparticles and its biothiols biosensing application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The graphitic carbon nitride nanodots (g-C3N4-dots) were synthesized by a simple electrochemical “tailoring” process from bulk graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) under alkaline solution for the first time. Compared with the bulk g-C3N4, the novel g-C3N4-dots not only exhibit enhanced fluorescence and excellent dispersion stability in water but also show the reducibility for the reduction of Ag+ to AgNPs at 60°C. The biothiols can bound with Ag+ through formation of biothiol-Ag+ complex to consume the Ag+ and act as capping agent to prevent the growth of AgNPs, which cause the decrease of the absorption peak of the AgNPs. Therefore, an optical sensor was developed for the detection of biothiols based on the change of the plasmon resonance absorption peak of the AgNPs. The proposed method exhibits excellent sensitivity and selectivity to biothiols with low detection limit for cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy), and glutathione (GSH) with 11.5, 16.1, and 15.5nM, respectively. This method also has been successfully applied for the detection of biothiols in human serum with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Low-molecular mass biological thiols, such as systeine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy) and glutathione (GSH), have been proved to play important roles in metabolism and maintain the appropriate redox status in cells (Meister and Anderson, 1983; Wood et al., 2003; Zhang et al., 2004). The abnormal level of these biothiols concentration in human body is correlated with many diseases. For example, high concentration of Cys have been associated with neurotoxicity (Wang and Cynader, 2001), and deficiency of Cys can lead to slowing growth, hematopoiesis decrease, psoriasis, etc (Shahrokhian, 2001; Townsend et al., 2003). An elevated concentration of Hcy is an important indicator of the cardiovascular diseases, dementia and Alzheimer's disease (Seshadri et al., 2002). GSH is an important signal of oxidative stress change in cell, and the abnormal concentration of GSH can lead the stroke, cancer, and many neurodegenerative disease (Kleinman and Richie Jr, 2000; Dalton et al., 1999; Zhang et al., 2005). Therefore, the detection of biological thiols in biological samples is very important, and a number of analytical methods have been developed to detect biological thiols with highly sensitive and selective in biological samples (Chen et al., 2010). Among these various detection techniques, optical detection has been proved to be the most convenient method due to its low cost and easy operation (Chen et al., 2010).

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), an important class of conjugated polymer semiconductors, possess fascinating electronic band structure, excellent chemical stability and environmental friendly, which have attracted tremendous researcher's attention in recent years (Gong et al., 2015). Bulk g-C3N4 can be prepared in large scale by thermal treatment of low-cost nitrogen-rich precursors, including melamine, cyanamide, urea and ammonium thiocyanate, etc (Zhao et al., 2015). The heptazine ring structure and the high condensation degree in g-C3N4 contribute to it not only has good physicochemical stability but also possesses an appealing electronic structure with a medium band gap of 2.7eV (Wang et al., 2009). These unique properties make g-C3N4 attractively for many applications, including visible light photocatalytic water splitting, photodegradation of environmental organic pollutants. Although g-C3N4 have been widely applicated as metal-free catalyst and catalyst support, the other potential values of g-C3N4 are not fully explored. Meanwhile, some disadvantages of the bulk structure of g-C3N4, such as the high recombination rate of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, the small specific surface area and insolubility in most solvent such as water, limit its applications in other areas. Compared with their bulk materials, quantum dots exhibit distinct physicochemical properties due to their quantum confinement effect and large specific surface area. Therefore, there are several reasons to believe that changing the bulk g-C3N4 into g-C3N4 nanodots (g-C3N4-dots) may endow promising properties. Thus, it is worthwhile developing a method to produce g-C3N4-dots to expand the potential application of g-C3N4.

Herein, we report a “top-down” method for the synthesis of g-C3N4-dots by simple electrochemical “tailoring” process of bulk g-C3N4 in alkaline solution. The method has the advantages of low-cost, easy operation and environmental friendly. The as-synthesized g-C3N4-dots exhibit enhanced fluorescence and excellent dispersion stability in water. Moreover, we find that the g-C3N4-dots show a novel property: the as-synthesized g-C3N4-dots can be used as reducing agent to reduce Ag+ to AgNPs. Meanwhile, due to the strong binding preference of biothiols toward Ag+ by forming a biothiol-Ag+ complex, the biothiols can also act as capping agent to prevent the growth of AgNPs, thus the amounts of as-synthesized AgNPs are decreased. Therefore, we develop a facile optical sensor for the detection of biothiols by monitoring the change of the absorption spectrum of the AgNPs. It also successfully applied to detect biothiols in human serum samples, and satisfied results are achieved.

Melamine, AgNO3 and NaOH were obtained from Shanghai Reagent (Shanghai, China). Cys, Hcy and GSH were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Other regents of analytical grade were obtained from Beijing Chemical Company (Beijing, China) and were used as received without any further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2MΩcm−1; Millpore., USA) was used throughout the experiments.

The UV–vis spectra and the fluorescence spectra (FL) were obtained using a UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Japan) and a F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Ltd, Japan), respectively. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected from a JEOL-1230 transmission electronic microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) in the 4000–400cm−1 regions were recorded on a Nicolet Nexus 670 FT-IR spectroscope (Nicolet Instrument Co., USA).

The bulk g-C3N4 were synthesized by direct thermal treatment of melamine. In a typical synthesis, 4g melamine was put into an alumina crucible with a cover and then heated from the room temperature to 550°C with a ramp rate of 3°Cmin−1 in static air, and then the temperature was kept at 550°C for 4h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was taken out and ground into fine powders in the agate mortar.

The g-C3N4-dots were synthesized by an electrochemical “tailoring” process. Briefly, 100mg bulk g-C3N4, 50mg sodium hydroxide and 10mL H2O mixed solution was used as the electrolytes, platinum sheets as the positive and the negative electrodes. The g-C3N4-dots were synthesized by electrolyzing electrolyte solution under 10V (DC) for 3h. After electrolysis, the electrolyte solution was dialyzed (Mw 1000) against water for over two days. Then, the product was centrifuged at a speed of 10,000rpm for 15min to remove the large size particles.

The AgNPs were synthesized as follows: a certain amount of AgNO3 aqueous solution was added to 1mL aqueous solution of the as-synthesized g-C3N4-dots solution (the final concentrations of AgNO3 and the g-C3N4-dots were 0.1mM and 0.04mgmL−1, respectively), and the pH was adjusted to 9 by NaOH. Then, the mixture solution was put into 60°C water bath for 30min. After that, cooled the reaction mixture with ice water to stop further reaction and the UV–vis spectra were recorded immediately.

100µL biothiols solution with different concentration were added into above g-C3N4-dots and Ag+ mixture solution and the mixture were thoroughly mixed. Then, the mixture solution was put into 60°C water bath for 30min After cooling the reaction mixture with ice water, the UV–vis spectra were recorded immediately.

The human serum samples were obtained from Hospital of Hunan Normal University (Changsha, China). All the experiments were conducted in compliance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines, and the relevant institutional committees have approved the experiments. According to the literature, the pretreatment of human serum samples were carried out (Hou et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2013). 1mL acetonitrile was added into 1mL the serum sample to eliminate the interferences of high proteins. After stirring for 5min, the mixture was centrifuged at 10 000rpm for 15min, and then the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22µm membrane filter for further sample analysis. Before measurement, the filtrate was diluted (1 %) for further analysis.

The bulk g-C3N4 were synthesized by direct thermal treatment of melamine and characterized by Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. As shown in Fig. 1(A), the strong band centered at 1249–1637cm−1 is correspond to the typical stretching vibration of heptazine heterocycle units, while the characteristic peak at 809cm−1 is assigned to the breathing mode of the heptazine heterocycle units (Liu et al., 2011). The FT-IR results indicate that the g-C3N4 are successfully synthesized. The typical transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the g-C3N4-dots synthesized by electrochemical tailoring of bulk g-C3N4 in alkaline solution is shown in Fig. 1(B). It shows that the g-C3N4-dots are mostly spherical dots with low multiple dispersity. The corresponding size distribution histogram obtained by counting about 100 particles indicates that these nanoparticles have diameters ranging from 5 to 8nm (Fig. S1). It shows that the g-C3N4-dots have been obtained. Since the voltage applied in this experiment is high enough to oxidize water to generate hydroxyl and oxygen radicals, as the electrochemical tailoring approach for synthesis of graphene quantum dots, the abundant hydroxyl and oxygen radical can be absorbed on bulk g-C3N4, then break the structure of g-C3N4 to obtain the g-C3N4-dots (Bao et al., 2011; Deng et al., 2015; Li et al., 2011; Shinde and Pillai, 2012; Zhang et al., 2012). The change of the structure of g-C3N4-dots was analyzed by FT-IR spectrum. As shown in Fig. 1(A), compared with the bulk g-C3N4, the peaks are almost no change after the electrochemical “tailoring” process, indicating that the structures of g-C3N4-dots are similar to those bulk g-C3N4. However, the new broad peak around at 3183 and 3436cm−1 suggests the existence of hydroxyl groups and amine groups of the g-C3N4-dots. In the process of alkaline hydrothermal treatment of g-C3N4, hydroxyl groups and amine groups were generated by the process of break the structure of g-C3N4 (Sano et al., 2013).

The remarkable optical properties of the as-synthesized g-C3N4-dots were characterized by the UV–vis spectra and the fluorescence (FL) spectra. As shown in Fig. S2, the as-synthesized g-C3N4-dots have a characteristic absorption peak at 268nm, which is typically attributable to the π-π* electronic transition in the heptazine heterocycle compounds. What's more, the FL spectrum shows an emission peak at 450nm when excited at 315nm (Fig. S2A). The detailed FL emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of the g-C3N4-dots are shown in Fig. S2B, As-synthesized g-C3N4-dots show an excitation-independent FL behavior, the emission peaks of g-C3N4-dots are not shifted with the excitation wavelength changing from 260 to 420nm. For further exploration of the optical properties of the as-synthesized g-C3N4-dots, a detailed FL study was undertaken in water with different pH values, containing NaCl of various ionic strengths and long time illumination (Fig. S3). As shown in Fig. S3A, the FL intensity of g-C3N4-dots is dependent on pH values and reaches the maximum FL intensity at pH 7.0. Fig. S3B shows the FL intensity of g-C3N4-dots does not change obviously even when the concentration of NaCl is 1.0M. Then, the FL intensity does not change even after continuous excitation with a 150W Xe lamp for 60min (Fig. S3C). Furthermore, the g-C3N4-dots still exhibit good photostability when stored at room temperature for a month (Fig. S3D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of AgNPs with g-C3N4-dots as reducing agent
ce:section.ce:para: Besides the excellent optical properties, the g-C3N4-dots also exhibit reducibility that can directly reduce Ag+ to AgNPs without additional reducing agent or photoirradiation at 60°C. When the as-synthesized g-C3N4-dots mixed with AgNO3 solution under pH 9, and incubated in water bath at 60°C, the color of the reaction mixture solution changed from colorless to yellow slowly. UV–vis absorption spectrum is recorded and it exhibits a significant absorption peak at 420nm, which matches well with the typical plasmon resonance absorption peak of AgNPs (Fig. 1(C)). However, no AgNPs are formed in the absence of g-C3N4-dots, which indicates that synthesized g-C3N4-dots have the ability to reduce Ag+ to AgNPs. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S4) of g-C3N4-dots shows a new peak at 1731cm−1, which are assigned to the stretching vibration of carbonyl groups, is appeared after reduction with Ag+. Moreover, the decreases of intensity of amine groups (3183cm−1) after the reduction imply that the consumption of hydroxyl groups and amine groups during the reduction process. As the reported carbon dots with abundant hydroxyl groups and amine groups, the reducibility may be related to the hydroxyl groups and amine groups of g-C3N4-dots (Lu et al., 2015; Shen et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2013).

TEM was used to characterize the morphology and size of as-synthesized AgNPs. As shown in Fig. 1(D), the AgNPs are nearly spherical with diameters ranging from 10 to 14nm (Fig. S5). The clear lattice spacing of 0.24nm on the HRTEM image display the message corresponding to the (111) lattice space of metallic Ag (JCPDS 04-0783). From the TEM image, we can see the low-contrast g-C3N4-dots wrap around the high-contrast AgNPs, suggesting that the g-C3N4-dots can help stabilize the as-synthesized AgNPs in solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of biothiols with g-C3N4-dots as reducing agent
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the strong binding preference of biothiols toward Ag+ and the strong Ag-S interaction between thiol group and AgNPs (Liu et al., 2012; Ran et al., 2013; Shen et al., 2011; Shen et al., 2014), we developed an optical sensor for biothiols by the change of the plasmon resonance absorption peak of the AgNPs. Due to the important role in biological processes and the importance for diagnosis of disease, Cys, Hcy and GSH were chosen as representative biothiols in this study. As shown in Fig. 2, when Cys is introduced, the absorption peak of the AgNPs obviously decreases. It has been reported that the Ag+-Cys complexes can be photoreduced to form AgNPs.(Liu et al., 2012) However, under the condition of proposed sensing system, Ag+-Cys complexes can not form AgNPs (Fig. S6). The absorbance of AgNPs decreased largely with the increasing of Cys concentration (Fig. 2), indicating that there should be some relationship between the concentration of Cys and the decreased absorbance of AgNPs. It also indicates that the quantitative detection of Cys is feasible by measuring the absorption peak intensity change of the AgNPs. Meanwhile, Hcy and GSH also show similar results (Fig. S7 and S8).

The proposed detection mechanism was depicted in Fig. 3, and summarized as follows: upon addition of biothiols, the growth of AgNPs is prevented because the Ag+ is consumed in the reaction solution through formation of biothiol-Ag+ complex. At the same time, the biothiols can be absorbed on the AgNPs surface due to the strong Ag-S interaction between thiol groups and AgNPs, which inhibit the growth of AgNPs. As a result, the amounts of as-synthesized AgNPs are decreasing and its corresponding plasmon resonance absorption peak of AgNPs is also decreasing. By measuring the absorption peak of the as-synthesized AgNPs, an optical sensor is developed for the detection of biothiols.

Generally, the greater change of the detection signal often leads higher detection sensitivity. In other word, in our proposed method, the concentration of the AgNPs will affect the detection sensitivity directly. Therefore, the concentration of the g-C3N4-dots and Ag+, which affect the synthesis of AgNPs, should be optimized. As shown in Fig. S9A, the absorption peak of AgNPs increases with the increasing of the concentration of the g-C3N4-dots, however, when the concentrations of g-C3N4-dots are too high, the absorption peak of AgNPs will decrease. Fig. S9B shows that the absorption peak of AgNPs increases with the increasing of the incubation time and did not undergo an obvious change after 30min, suggesting that the reaction could finish within 30min As shown in Fig. S10A, the maximum absorbance decrease rate ((A 0-A)/A 0) is obtained at the concentration of g-C3N4-dots at 0.04mgmL−1. When the concentration of the Ag+ is same, the higher concentration of g-C3N4-dots, the more Ag+ are adsorbed on the surface of g-C3N4-dots and the concentration of free Ag+ in solution is decreased. Consequently, during the reduction process, there is no continuous free Ag+ from the solution to be reduced to Ag(0) and aggregate into nanocrystals by self nucleation. As a result, the growth of AgNPs is partially blocked and number of AgNPs is decreased (Shen et al., 2013; Xia et al., 2009). Therefore, we choose the concentration of g-C3N4-dots at 0.04mgmL−1 as the optimal detection experiments. Fig. S10B shows the influence of Ag+ concentration to the absorbance decrease rate. It is observed that the absorbance decrease rate is obtained at the concentration of Ag+ at 0.1mM. Therefore, the concentration of Ag+ at 0.1mM is used in this method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The analytical performance of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal experiment conditions, the analytical performances of the proposed method for biothiols were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4(A), the absorbance peak intensity of the AgNPs is decreased with the increasing of the concentration of Cys, and the corresponding relationship between the absorbance decrease rate of AgNPs and the concentration of Cys is shown in Fig. 4(B). Because signal drift (cause by scattering, photo-bleaching, dye leaching and so on) often occurring within optical chemical sensors, the correction for signal drift is necessary. Recently, Hakonen and co-workers proposed a strategy that is square root calibration function to simplify the calibration process and satisfactory results are achieved (Hakonen and Stromberg, 2012). Herein, we followed this method reported by Hakonen. In the range of 0.02–20µM, the absorbance decrease rate undergoes a square root change with respect to the concentration of Cys, and the square root calibration equation is y = 0.158√x + 0.055 (R 2=0.9915) where y and x are the absorbance decrease rate ((A 0-A)/A 0) and the concentration of Cys, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) is the concentration of the analyte which produces an analytical signal equal to 3 times of the standard deviation of the blank measurements, which is calculated to be 11.5nM. Meanwhile, Hcy and GSH also show similar absorbance peak intensity changes in the range of 0.02–20µM. For Hcy, the corresponding calibration equation and LOD is y = 0.174√x + 0.043 (R 2=0.9985) and 16.1nM, respectively. For GSH, the corresponding calibration equation and LOD is y = 0.174√x + 0.020 (R 2=0.9960) and 15.5nM, respectively (Fig. S11 and S12). For comparison, some of colorimetric assay methods for biothiols detection are shown in Table S1, (Chen et al., 2009; Lee et al., 2008; Li and Li, 2009, Li et al., 2009; Ma et al., 2012; Xiao et al., 2011) suggesting that the proposed method exhibits superior sensitivity for biothiols detection.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of the proposed method for the determination of biothiols, some other amino acids, main metal ions and other molecules in biological fluids were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5, the absorbance change rate caused by biothiols is larger than other compounds, which indicates that the method has excellent selectivity toward biothiols over other compounds. It seems that the excellent selectivity can be attributed to the strong interaction between biothiols and Ag+/AgNPs. It is notice that the proteins which contain cysteine residues (such as BSA) show higher interference. However, in practical assays, the potential interference from proteins can be eliminated by appropriate pretreatment technology to precipitate proteins and this method will be a promising method to detect biothiols in practical samples (Hou et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2013).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Cys in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above results, we further validated the practicality of the proposed method in human serum samples. A standard addition method is used to analyze concentrations of biothiols in human serum, and we select Cys as the standard, because it is the most abundant thiol compound in human serum (Ran et al., 2013; Sreejith et al., 2008). The results are summarized in Table 1. As shown in Table 1, excellent recovery in the range from 95.33% to 105.25% is obtained, indicating the proposed method is reliable and suitable for practical applications.

In summary, the g-C3N4-dots have been successfully synthesized by a simple electrochemical “tailoring” process from bulk g-C3N4 under alkaline solution. This method has the advantages of low cost and environmental friendly. The as-synthesized g-C3N4-dots show high water-solubility and high FL. Further study confirms that the as-synthesized g-C3N4-dots can act as reducing agent to reduce Ag+ to AgNPs at 60°C. Based on this novel property and the strong interaction between biothiols and Ag+, an optical sensor has been developed for biothiols detection. The proposed method displays high sensitivity and excellent selectivity for the determination of biothiols and has been successfully applied to practical sample detection in human serum samples. We provide a new approach for the sensing application of g-C3N4.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21275051, 21475043), Doctoral Fund of Ministry of Education of China (No: 20134306110006), Science and Technology Department of Hunan Province (No. 2014FJ6034) and the Hunan Provincial Innovation Foundation for Postgraduate (CX2014B223).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at 10.1016/j.bios.2016.05.064.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
